spec_match.rationale: Compares the four DBSC key-storage tiers (software, TEE, TPM 2.0, Secure Enclave / Strongbox) across attack classes, which is exactly the threat model the webappsec-dbsc spec uses to motivate hardware binding.

interactive.rationale: Four tier buttons drive a live score matrix against four attack classes, with scores updating per selection and concrete-attack rationale text appearing for each.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature blurb sells 'compare software-only fallback, TEE, TPM 2.0, Secure Enclave across four attack classes' — page implements exactly that with a tier × attack matrix.

edge_cases.rationale: Tiers and attack classes are covered. Missing: the 'no key store at all' degraded mode (when policy denies storage), and the cross-keystore migration story (key rotation between tiers when a user changes devices).

references.rationale: The see-also list now includes the required ChromeStatus feature 5140168270413824 plus the webappsec-dbsc spec.

design_system.rationale: Tier-selector buttons override background+color together; matrix cells use --accent-emerald/amber/rose; no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Add a key-rotation / device-migration scenario
openQuestions.detail: When a user gets a new device, DBSC has to re-bind the session to the new keystore. The matrix today is static (one tier at a time); a 'migrate from tier X to tier Y' button would surface the cross-tier handshake which is one of the operational pain points the spec acknowledges.

openQuestions.title: Cite the attack-class sources
openQuestions.detail: The 'concrete attacks each tier defeats' text reads authoritatively but doesn't link to threat-model sources (e.g. SOK papers on TPM attacks, public Strongbox / Secure Enclave docs). Add citation links under each attack-class column header so authors can dig in.

summary: Strong threat-model explainer with a real matrix UI and the required ChromeStatus link restored. The next useful addition is a tier-migration scenario for the device-swap edge case.
